title: Supporting the designation of March 2025 as National Colorectal Cancer Awareness Month.
plain_summary: This bill asks Congress to make March 2025 National Colorectal Cancer Awareness Month. The goal is to teach people about colorectal cancer and how to prevent it. The bill says this type of cancer can often be prevented if people get checked by doctors early enough. However, many Americans are not getting the medical tests they need to find this cancer early. The special month would help spread the word about why these health screenings are important.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

sections.title: Resolution Supporting the Designation of March 2025 as National Colorectal Cancer Awareness Month
sections.text: This resolution supports making March two thousand twenty-five National Colorectal Cancer Awareness Month. Colorectal cancer affects the colon and rectum, which are parts of the large intestine. This type of cancer is the second biggest cause of cancer deaths for both men and women together in the United States. Colorectal cancer can often be prevented because doctors can find and remove small growths called polyps before they turn into cancer. When doctors find colorectal cancer early through screening tests, treatments work much better. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention says that if everyone fifty years old or older got regular screening tests, up to sixty percent of deaths from colorectal cancer could be stopped. When colorectal cancer is found early and has not spread to other parts of the body, ninety percent of patients live at least five years after diagnosis. However, only thirty-nine percent of all colorectal cancer cases are found at this early stage. Even though screening tests work well to reduce colorectal cancer cases and deaths, one in three adults over fifty years old are not getting the screening tests they should. One in twenty-three men and one in twenty-six women will get colorectal cancer during their lifetime. By the year two thousand thirty, colorectal cancer is expected to become the top cancer killer for people between twenty and forty-nine years old. Every March, groups run public awareness and education campaigns about preventing colorectal cancer, getting screened, and knowing the warning signs. These educational efforts help teach people about ways to prevent the disease, how to get screened, and what symptoms to watch for so cancer can be found early. Therefore, the House of Representatives supports the goals and ideas of National Colorectal Cancer Awareness Month and encourages all Americans to participate in awareness and educational activities during March.
